questions.question: Your Goblin Rabble horde has Speed 5 and no rules that modify its charge range. What is the greatest distance to an enemy at which it can legally declare a Charge!?
questions.explanation: A charge can be declared against a target up to double the unit's Speed away, so 5 x 2 = 10". The tempting 5" is the Advance! distance (single Speed); charging lets you reach twice as far.

questions.question: Your Big Rocks Thrower is a War Engine. Which one of these Movement orders can it NOT be given?
options: Halt!
Change Facing!
At the Double!
Back!
questions.explanation: War Engines cannot be ordered to move At the Double! (nor to Charge!). They can still Halt!, Change Facing!, Advance!, Back! and Sidestep! normally, which is why the other three are all legal.

questions.question: Your Goblins are Engaged and you order them to Withdraw! They pass the Nerve test. After turning to face the enemy, how far do they then move directly backwards?
options: Half their Speed
Their full Speed
questions.explanation: A successful Withdraw! moves the unit 2D6" straight back (unaffected by Difficult Terrain and Obstacles). Half-Speed is the Back! order, not Withdraw!, which is a common mix-up.

questions.question: Your Goblin Spitters need 5+ to hit. This Turn they were given a Halt! order (so they Aim), and their target is in cover. What do they need to roll to hit?
questions.explanation: Aiming (from a Halt! order) is +1 and cover is -1; the two modifiers cancel, leaving the base 5+. The tempting 4+ only counts the Aim bonus and forgets the cover penalty.

questions.question: An enemy regiment is Engaged in Combat with one of your Goblin units. Can your Goblin Spitters shoot at that enemy regiment if it is in range and line of sight?
options: No — units that are Engaged cannot be targeted by ranged attacks
Yes, if in range and LOS
Yes, but at -1 to hit
Only during your Combat Phase
questions.explanation: Engaged units cannot be chosen as targets for ranged attacks at all, so range and LOS are irrelevant here. It is not simply a to-hit penalty — the shot is illegal.
questions.source: Rulebook, The Ranged Phase — Combat and Ranged Attacks

questions.question: Your Goblin Spitters are standing inside a wood (agreed as Difficult Terrain that gives cover). They shoot an enemy unit out in the open, with clear line of sight and nothing between the two units. Does the enemy count as being in cover?
options: No — cover depends on the target's position, not the shooter's
Yes, because a wood is involved
Yes, whenever you shoot out of cover
Only if you fail a 4+ roll
questions.explanation: Cover is decided by terrain/units around or in front of the target, not the firer. A unit even ignores Difficult Terrain its own centre-point sits in when checking cover. The wood the shooters stand in gives the target nothing.

questions.question: Your Goblins have Sh 5+ and 10 ranged Attacks. After all modifiers they would need a 7+ to hit this target — impossible on a D6. What happens?
options: They cannot shoot at all
They roll 5 dice (half, rounding down), hitting on 6s
They roll all 10 dice, hitting on 6s
They roll 10 dice, needing 7 (so nothing hits)
questions.explanation: If modifiers make hitting impossible, the unit may still shoot, needs a 6 to hit, but rolls only half its Attacks (rounding down) — 5 dice here. A natural 1 is always a miss regardless.

questions.question: Your Goblin Rabble is Engaged with an enemy unit's flank and directs 20 Attacks at it. How many attack dice do you actually roll?
questions.explanation: Attacks against a flank are doubled before rolling to hit: 20 becomes 40. The tempting 60 is the rear multiplier (trebled), not the flank one.

questions.question: Your Goblins charge into combat and their move had to cross a low hedge (agreed as an Obstacle). What effect does crossing the Obstacle have on their attacks in the ensuing Combat?
options: No effect
-1 to hit (Hindered)
-1 to damage
They cannot complete the charge
questions.explanation: A charge that passes through or ends on Difficult Terrain or an Obstacle is Hindered: a single -1 to hit that Combat. Obstacles don't stop charges (that is the 'cannot charge' trap), and the penalty is to hit, not to damage. Strider/Reform! avoid it.
questions.source: Rulebook, The Combat Phase — Hindered Charges

questions.question: During your Combat Phase, your Goblins are Engaged with an enemy regiment. Whose units actually roll attacks in this Combat?
options: Both units strike simultaneously
Whichever unit charged
Only your units this Turn; the enemy strikes back on their own Turn
The unit with the higher Attacks value
questions.explanation: In your Turn only your Engaged units strike; the enemy defends and will get to hit back in their own Combat Phase. Expecting the enemy to roll back immediately is one of the most common beginner misplays.

questions.question: You have damaged an enemy unit and now test its Nerve. What do you roll, and what do you add before comparing to its Nerve value?
options: Roll 2D6 only
Roll D6 and add the damage
Roll 2D6 and add the damage currently on the unit
Roll 3D6
questions.explanation: You roll 2D6 and add the unit's current total damage (plus any special-rule modifiers). If that total reaches or exceeds the Nerve value, the test is failed.

questions.question: One of your Goblin units is Wavering at the start of your Turn. Which Movement orders may it be given?
options: Any order
Any order except Charge!
Only Halt!, Change Facing! or Back!
Halt! only
questions.explanation: A Wavering unit may only be given Halt!, Change Facing! or Back!, and it cannot make ranged attacks that Turn. It's more restricted than 'anything but Charge!', but it isn't frozen to Halt! alone.
questions.source: Rulebook, Nerve — Effects of a Failed Test (Wavering)

questions.question: You roll to test an enemy unit's Nerve and get double 1 (snake eyes). What happens?
options: The enemy automatically Routs
You re-roll both dice
The enemy automatically passes, ignoring all modifiers
Nothing special happens
questions.explanation: Double 1 is 'Hold Your Ground!' — the target passes the Nerve test automatically no matter how much damage or how many modifiers apply. A frequent source of frustration when trying to break a stubborn unit.
questions.source: Rulebook, Nerve — Exceptional Morale Results (Hold Your Ground!)

questions.question: Your Goblins are Engaged and you order Withdraw! They FAIL the Nerve test. What is the result?
options: They move back D6" but survive
They stay Engaged where they are
They are immediately Routed and removed
They Waver but remain on the table
questions.explanation: Failing the Withdraw! Nerve test means the unit breaks in confusion and is immediately Routed and removed — there is no half-measure. Any enemies it was fighting simply stay put.

questions.question: Your Goblin regiment is Engaged ONLY by a single enemy Individual (a lone hero). You order it to Withdraw! What happens with the Nerve test?
options: You take a normal 2D6 Nerve test
It passes automatically
It fails automatically
It cannot Withdraw! from an Individual
questions.explanation: A non-Individual unit that is Engaged only by Individuals automatically passes the Nerve test when it Withdraws! — so it always escapes. This is handy against lone chaff-catchers.

questions.question: Your Goblin Wiz casts an ordinary (non-Magical-Missile) spell at an enemy in cover. What does the spell normally need to hit, and does the cover matter?
options: It uses the caster's Sh stat, modified by cover
4+ to hit, but cover still applies
5+ to hit always
4+ to hit, ignoring all normal to-hit modifiers such as cover
questions.explanation: Most spells hit on a flat 4+ and ignore the normal ranged to-hit modifiers, so cover does nothing to an ordinary spell. Spells marked 'Magical Missile' are the exception — see the Magical Missile question.

questions.question: Your Goblin Wiz moved At the Double! this Turn. Can it still cast a spell in the Ranged Phase?
options: Yes, spells are exempt from movement rules
No — spells are ranged attacks, and moving At the Double! prevents ranged attacks
Only Magical Missiles can be cast
Yes, but at -1 to hit
questions.explanation: Spells follow the ranged rules, so a unit that moved At the Double! (too busy marching) cannot cast. Remember a unit may make only one ranged attack per Turn anyway, so it can't cast two spells either.

questions.question: A spell in your list is marked 'Magical Missile' and its target is in cover. Does the -1 cover modifier apply to your to-hit roll?
options: Yes — Magical Missiles hit on 4+ but normal to-hit modifiers still apply
No, all spells ignore to-hit modifiers
No, but the spell loses its Piercing
Only if the caster moved this Turn
questions.explanation: Magical Missiles are the deliberate exception: they still hit on 4+, but unlike ordinary spells they DO suffer the usual to-hit modifiers like cover. Forgetting this is a common misplay in either direction.

questions.question: At what point during a unit's move can a Command Order be issued to or from it?
options: Only before the unit starts moving
Only after the unit finishes its whole move
Only at the very start of the Movement Phase
At any point during the move, but never mid-pivot (finish the turn first)
questions.explanation: A Command Order may be issued at any point during a unit's move — by the issuing or receiving unit — except in the middle of a pivot, which must be completed first.

questions.question: You have several commanders on the table. Must they all issue their Command Orders at the same moment?
options: No — each commander issues independently, whenever they want during the phase
Yes, all at once
Only one commander may issue an order per Turn
Yes, in a fixed order
questions.explanation: Each commander is free to issue a Command Order separately, at whatever point in the phase suits them; they don't all fire simultaneously.

questions.question: A unit has Speed 5 and the Wild Charge (2) special rule. What is its maximum charge range?
questions.explanation: Wild Charge (n) is added AFTER Speed is doubled: (5 x 2) + 2 = 12". The 14" trap doubles the total after adding (i.e. (5+2) x 2), and 7" forgets to double at all.

questions.question: The Vicious special rule lets a unit re-roll which dice?
options: All dice that failed to hit
Every damage die, once
Dice that roll a 1 when rolling to hit
Dice that roll a natural 1 when rolling to damage
questions.explanation: Vicious re-rolls natural, unmodified 1s when rolling to DAMAGE (and can be limited to a phase, e.g. Vicious (Combat), which the Mawpup Launcher grants your Green Horde). Re-rolling failed to-hit dice is a different rule (Elite).

questions.question: Your Goblin units carry the 'Green Horde' Trait. On its own, what does having a Trait like this do?
options: Grants +1 Nerve
Nothing directly — it just marks the unit as a target for rules, spells or Command Orders that reference that Trait
Gives a free re-roll each Turn
Adds extra Attacks
questions.explanation: Traits have no game effect by themselves; they only flag a unit so that other rules can single it out — e.g. the Mawpup Launcher can target friendly Green Horde units. That's why Green Horde lives in traits, not special_rules.

questions.question: A friendly unit within 6" of your Inspiring Goblin hero fails a Nerve test (a Waver or Rout result). What does Inspiring do?
options: The unit passes the test automatically
The Nerve test must be re-rolled, and the second result stands
The hero suffers the damage instead
Nothing — Inspiring only protects the hero itself
questions.explanation: Inspiring forces a re-roll of a Waver/Rout result for itself and friendly units within 6" (Very Inspiring reaches 9"); the second roll stands. It doesn't guarantee a pass. Note a 'We Are Doomed!' from Combat can't be re-rolled this way.

questions.question: For Line of Sight and cover, what Height does a War Engine (such as a Sharpstick Thrower) have by default?
questions.explanation: War Engines are Height 2, the same as Infantry. Cavalry are 3 and Monsters are 5 — don't assume a big machine is tall.
questions.source: Rulebook, Arcs and Line of Sight — Unit Height

questions.question: A Goblin unit's profile lists its Attacks as 'D6+8'. How many attack dice do you roll?
options: Roll one D6 and add 8 to the result
Roll 8 dice
Roll 6 dice and add 8
Roll 14 dice
questions.explanation: 'D6+n' means roll a single D6 and add n, so D6+8 gives 9–14 attacks that Turn. It is not six dice plus eight, nor a flat 14.

questions.question: Can your Goblins move At the Double! straight through an area of Difficult Terrain, such as a wood?
options: No — while moving At the Double!, Difficult Terrain counts as Blocking Terrain (go around it)
Yes, with no penalty
Yes, but only at half Speed
Yes, but the unit is Hindered
questions.explanation: At the Double! treats Difficult Terrain as Blocking, so the unit must go around. A normal Advance! can move through it (and Pathfinder ignores the penalty entirely). The crux here is the terrain rule, not the order's base distance.

questions.question: A low hedge is agreed to be an Obstacle. Under a normal Advance! order, can your Goblin infantry move across it?
options: Yes — units cross Obstacles normally on a normal move (and may even end on top)
No, Obstacles block all movement
Only War Engines may cross Obstacles
Only units with Fly may cross
questions.explanation: Obstacles are crossed normally during ordinary moves and can be ended upon; only At the Double! treats them as Blocking. War Engines are the exception — THEY treat Obstacles as Blocking — which is the reverse of the trap answer.

questions.question: As it turns, can a unit pivot through a piece of Blocking Terrain (like a building), provided it ends clear of it?
options: No, Blocking Terrain stops pivots as well
Only if the unit has Fly
Yes — a unit may pivot through Blocking Terrain, friends and enemies, but must END the pivot and its whole move clear of them
Only War Engines can do this
questions.explanation: When pivoting on its centre, a unit can swing through Blocking Terrain and other units, as long as the finished position is clear. A straight move (forward/back/sideways), by contrast, cannot cross Blocking Terrain.
questions.source: Rulebook, The Movement Phase — Interpenetration When Pivoting

questions.question: In the basic game, at the end of Round 6, how is it decided whether a Round 7 is played?
options: The player who went second rolls a die: 4-6 = a Round 7 is played, 1-3 = the game ends
The game always ends after Round 6
The game always continues to Round 7
The player who went first chooses
questions.explanation: After Round 6 the second player rolls: on a 4-6 both players take one more Turn (Round 7), on a 1-3 the game ends. This dice roll, not a fixed length, is what determines the final Turn.

questions.question: Your Goblins have Speed 6 and pick up a Loot Counter. What is their Speed while carrying it?
options: 6 (unchanged)
questions.explanation: Carrying a Loot Counter caps a unit's Speed at 5 (unless it was already below 5). The crux is the Loot Counter rule, not the printed Speed — which is why this is a scenario question even though the effect lands on movement.

questions.question: In the basic 'Playing a Game' scenario, how is the winner decided at the end?
options: Whoever destroyed the most points of enemy units
The player with the most units still on the table wins
Whoever controls the centre of the board
The player with the most individual models left
questions.explanation: Basic victory goes to whoever has the most surviving units; if tied, the player who still has their hero wins, otherwise it's a draw. It counts units, not models or points — which quietly favours a numerous Goblin list.

questions.question: Enemy shooting inflicts damage on your Goblin Sharpsticks regiment (Ne 16) and it fails the Nerve test. What is the result?
options: It is Routed and removed
Nothing — shooting cannot break a unit
It becomes Wavering until the end of its next Turn
It falls back D6" but survives
questions.explanation: A Nerve test failed because of ranged damage causes Wavering, not a Rout. The unit stays on the table but next Turn can only be given Halt!, Change Facing! or Back! and cannot shoot. Only combat-caused fails Rout a unit outright.
questions.source: Rulebook, Nerve — Effects of a Failed Test (Wavering)

questions.question: In your opponent's Combat Phase your Goblin Rabble regiment (Ne 14) takes damage and fails its Nerve test. What happens?
options: It becomes Wavering but stays
It retreats 2D6" and survives
It passes automatically because it is a horde
It is Routed and removed from the table
questions.explanation: A Nerve test failed because of combat damage means the unit is Routed — destroyed and removed. The same die total that would only Waver a unit from shooting Routs it in combat; the difference is the source of the damage, since the Nerve value itself is single.
questions.source: Rulebook, Nerve — Effects of a Failed Test (Routed)

questions.question: Your Goblin Spitters regiment (Ne 15) has taken so much damage that its total now EXCEEDS its Nerve value, making it Devastated. Enemy shooting then makes it fail another Nerve test. Normally a shooting fail only Wavers a unit — what happens here?
options: It Wavers as usual
It automatically passes while Devastated
It regains a point of damage and Wavers
It is Routed, because a Devastated unit treats Waver results as Routs
questions.explanation: Once damage exceeds a unit's Nerve value it is Devastated: it halves its Attacks AND treats any Waver result as a Rout. So even a shooting-caused fail now removes it, including the very test taken on becoming Devastated. Goblins tip over this line quickly.

questions.question: When drawing Line of Sight from your unit to a target, from which point(s) do you draw the imaginary line?
options: From any point of your unit's front facing to any point of the target's base
From your unit's centre-point to the target's centre-point
From the closest point of your unit to the closest point of the target
From your unit's rear facing to the target's front facing
questions.explanation: LOS is drawn from any point on your unit's front facing to any point on the target's base -- it doesn't have to be the shortest line, and centre-point-to-centre-point is a common but incorrect shortcut.
questions.source: Rulebook, Arcs and Line of Sight -- Drawing LOS

questions.question: Your Sharpstick Thrower has no special rule overriding its range. What is its Ranged range?
questions.explanation: Unless otherwise specified, War Engines have a range of 48". The Sharpstick Thrower's Piercing (1) doesn't change its range -- only named exceptions like the War-Trombone (12") or Mawpup Launcher (36") override the default.

questions.question: Per the Unit Height table, what Height is your King on Chariot for Line of Sight purposes?
options: Height (2)
Height (3)
Height (4)
Height (5)
questions.explanation: Chariots have a Height of 4 by unit type -- one taller than Cavalry (3), one shorter than Monsters (5). Height governs whether your model blocks or is blocked by intervening units and terrain.
questions.source: Rulebook, Arcs and Line of Sight -- Unit Height

questions.question: Your unit has suffered enough damage to exceed its Nerve value but hasn't yet Routed. What happens to its Attacks stat?
options: Its Attacks stat is halved, rounding down, for both Combat and ranged attacks
Nothing changes until it actually fails a Nerve test
It loses all further ranged attacks but Combat is unaffected
Its Melee and Shooting values each worsen by 1
questions.explanation: Once damage exceeds a unit's Nerve value it's Devastated: Attacks (including ranged and Magical Missile (n) values) are halved, rounding down, immediately -- not just after a failed Nerve test.

questions.question: Your Wiz's profile lists its Shooting (Sh) stat as '-'. What does this tell you?
options: It automatically hits with ranged attacks
It can never be the target of ranged attacks
It needs a 6 to hit with ranged attacks
It has no normal ranged attacks
questions.explanation: A '-' in the Melee or Shooting stat simply means the unit has no normal attacks of that kind -- for the Wiz, its only 'ranged' output comes from its Spellcaster trait's own spells, not a Sh-based attack.

questions.question: Your Wiz (an Individual) is Engaged in combat. Can it use the Individuals rule to pivot for free before receiving its order?
options: No -- the free pivot only applies to Individuals that are not Engaged
Yes, Individuals can always pivot for free
Yes, but only up to 45 degrees
No, Individuals can never pivot, Engaged or not
questions.explanation: The Individuals special rule grants a free pivot before orders 'unless they are Engaged' -- so an Engaged Individual loses that exception and is restricted to Halt!, Reform!, or Withdraw! like any other Engaged unit.

questions.question: Under an Advance! order, how many separate pivots (each up to 90 degrees) can your unit make during its move?
options: None
One
Up to two
Up to three
questions.explanation: Advance! allows up to two pivots of up to 90 degrees each, at any point in the move. That's more generous than At the Double! or a Charge!, which each only allow a single pivot.

questions.question: During a Charge! move, how many pivots can the charging unit make, and how large can each be?
options: None
Two pivots, up to 90 degrees each
One pivot, up to 180 degrees
One pivot, up to 90 degrees
questions.explanation: A charging unit gets a single pivot of up to 90 degrees from its original facing at any point during the move. If that one pivot isn't enough to bring it into contact with the target's facing, the charge cannot be completed.
questions.source: Rulebook, The Movement Phase -- Charge! -- Moving Chargers

questions.question: Your unit has the Radiance of Life special rule. In which phase does it heal damage from friendly units within 6"?
options: The Movement Phase, after the unit completes its order
The Ranged Phase, before shooting
The Combat Phase, after attacks are resolved
Any phase, chosen by the controlling player
questions.explanation: Radiance of Life triggers in the Movement Phase, after the unit has completed its order: it and all friendly units within 6" remove one point of previously suffered damage. A unit can only benefit from one source of Radiance of Life per Turn.
questions.source: Full Rulebook, Special Rules -- Radiance of Life

questions.question: Your Spellcaster's profile lists three different spells it knows. How many of them can it cast in a single Ranged Phase?
options: One
Two
All three, one after another
As many as its Attacks stat allows
questions.explanation: Spells are Ranged attacks, and a unit can only make a single Ranged attack per Turn. Knowing multiple spells gives flexibility in which one to cast, not the ability to cast more than one.

questions.question: A spell is marked 'Friendly' but does not list 'Self' in its profile. Can the caster target itself with it?
options: Yes, Friendly always includes the caster
Only if the caster is Wavering
Only if there are no other legal friendly targets
No -- without 'Self' listed, a Friendly spell cannot target the caster
questions.explanation: Friendly spells can only target friendly units, and can only target the caster itself if 'Self' is also listed on the spell's profile. Without that qualifier, the caster is excluded from its own spell.

questions.question: Your unit is Engaged simultaneously by two enemy units, to its front and to its flank. How must it handle its attacks?
options: It must direct all attacks at a single enemy
Attacks are split automatically, evenly, by the game
It can only attack the unit that charged it
It can split its attacks between the two enemies as it chooses, before rolling any dice
questions.explanation: The controlling player freely distributes attacks among the enemies a unit is Engaged with, deciding the split before any dice are rolled. Bonuses like the flank/rear doubling/trebling are then applied separately to each portion.

questions.question: You have three separate Combats on the table in your Combat Phase. In what order must you resolve them?
options: Alphabetically by unit name
In the order they occurred during the game
Whatever order you like, but each unit's attacks must be resolved fully before moving to the next
Smallest unit first
questions.explanation: You choose the order in which to resolve your Combats, and within a Combat you choose the order of your own units' attacks -- but each unit's attacks must be fully resolved before moving on to the next.

questions.question: Modifiers against your unit in Combat are so severe that it could no longer hit even with the maximum possible roll. What happens?
options: The unit cannot attack at all this Combat
It attacks normally, ignoring the modifiers
It still attacks, needs a 6 to hit, but only rolls half its Attacks (rounding down)
It automatically hits once regardless
questions.explanation: Even when modifiers would make hitting impossible at the normal target number, the unit can still attack: it needs 6s to hit and rolls only half its Attacks stat, rounding down. It's never simply locked out of attacking.

questions.question: Following the standard terrain height guidelines, what Height are Woods and Buildings typically assigned?
options: Height (2)
Height (3)
Height (5)
Height (8)
questions.explanation: The guideline table assigns Woods and Buildings Height (8) -- well above Hills (3) and High Hills (5) -- reflecting how effectively they block Line of Sight. As always, agree actual heights with your opponent before the game.
questions.source: Rulebook, Playing a Game -- Terrain and Line of Sight

questions.question: Part of your unit's base is standing inside an area of terrain. Does that terrain block Line of Sight to that part of your unit?
options: Yes, always
No -- terrain does not block LOS to the part of a unit's own base that's within it
Only if the terrain is Blocking Terrain
Only if the unit is Infantry
questions.explanation: If a unit has part of its base in an area of terrain, that terrain doesn't block Line of Sight to that part of the unit -- it only affects sight lines to points beyond it.
questions.source: Rulebook, Terrain and Line of Sight -- Drawing Line of Sight

questions.question: You won the roll-off and chose to deploy first, placing the first unit on the table. Does this also mean you get the first Turn?
options: Yes, deploying first automatically grants the first Turn
No -- 'who goes first' is decided by a separate roll-off after deployment is complete
Only if you also won the terrain-placement roll-off
Yes, unless your opponent has a Scout unit
questions.explanation: Deployment order and Turn order come from two separate roll-offs. After both armies are fully deployed, the players roll off again, and the winner chooses whether to take the first Turn or hand it to their opponent.
questions.source: Rulebook, Playing a Game -- Deployment / Who Goes First?

questions.question: During deployment, how far from the table's centre line must your units be placed?
options: At least 6"
More than 12"
Exactly 24"
There is no minimum distance
questions.explanation: Units are deployed on their own long edge of the battlefield, more than 12" away from the table's centre line, alternating placement with your opponent until both armies are fully deployed.

questions.question: A Command Order states that a unit 'cannot be given a Charge! order' this Turn. Can that unit still be moved into Combat by a spell like Surge?
options: No, the restriction blocks any way of entering Combat
Yes -- a restriction on being given a Charge! order only limits charges declared in the Movement Phase
Only if the unit is Fearless
No, unless the caster is the unit's own Commander
questions.explanation: A Command Order (or other rule) that prevents a Charge! order only restricts that order in the Movement Phase. It doesn't stop the unit being moved into Combat by other means, such as being Surged in during the Ranged Phase.

questions.question: Your unit is carrying a Loot Counter and is ordered to Withdraw! from combat. What happens to the Loot Counter?
options: It stays with the unit through the Withdraw!
The unit must drop it at the start of the Withdraw! order
It's automatically returned to the enemy
Nothing -- only Individuals are affected by this rule
questions.explanation: A unit that Withdraws! must drop any Loot Counter it's carrying at the start of that order -- you can't retreat and keep the loot in the same move.
